sentences.text: No, no, no, no.
Nothing at all, no.
Nothing like it.
No.
Just ourselves.
We were just trying-- everybody was trying to mind their own business and to try-- try not to volunteer for anything.
Only obey orders.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember-- I remember an incident in Ohrdruf that we were taken out.
We were taken out one day to clean the barracks of some officers.
Three young SS officers took us out.
I think there were about 16 of us.
And I don't remember how we got there, but only have the recollection of working there all day, cleaning up, without any food.
And then after we were finished working, we were going back to-- the SS were taking us back to camp, and they put us three abreast, marching us into town.
And they told-- they gave us orders, strict orders, that we have to keep in step.
And one of the SS said, anybody that is going to be out of step is going to get a number.
And when I give you a number, I'll take you to a place where I'm going to ask you to step out when I call your number.
So I think he had six people designated to come [? out ?] numbers.
My number was number 5.
I know what happened to me when I got in there, but I only know that the five people that preceded me, three of them had to be carried out with broken backs.
I came in there when he called my number, number 5.
I went in, and they were sitting by a desk, the three of them--
--with shovel handles in their hand.
And he asked me, do you know why you're here?
I says, yes.
He says, why?
I said, because I was out of step.
He says, are you afraid?
I says, no.
He says, OK, get out.
They wanted to see people pleading for their life.
And that's what they enjoyed.
And the more you pleaded for your life, the more they beat you.
And this is why three of the people that preceded me were carried out of there with broken backs.
And that was the end of them.
They carried them-- they had to carry  them into camp, to put them in to the-- to the sick-- sick section.
And that was the end of them.
That was an incident that happened in Ohrdruf.
Yeah.
When they took us to work in the morning, when we were marching through town, there were-- I don't know the name of the town, but something like [?
Erfurt ?] or [?
Weimar ?] is sticking in my mind.
And I remember looking at the houses with their lights in there, and the-- and the chimneys smoking.
And I thought to myself, my gosh, do these people realize what they are doing to us, that they are treating us like animals?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was just a thought.
And the thing is, that still and all, with all this torture and all this beating, we had hope.
We had hope for survival, because the minute you gave up hope-- there are people there that were working outside that you saw-- you saw they had they-- they had fever.
They started eating snow.
And you know that their hope is gone.
They had absolutely no hope, and they just dropped like flies.
I mean, the minute you gave up hope in camp, you were a dead person.
No.
Not in our case.
We were working with civilian engineers in stone quarries.
But we had absolutely no contact with them at all.
No, nothing.
We were not sustained with them.
We were not-- they were not-- they were not-- nothing.
I mean, they were just, we were just labor there for them, forced labor.
Well, the fences.
The fences-- and the-- the fences-- the camp was surrounded by fences, with the dogs on the outside of the fence.
We heard the barkings, and the towers-- the towers that were lit up at night.

And we were happy when we were in the barracks and they left us alone, because we knew when we-- when we had to get up in the morning, when they were raus, raus, raus, they were beating us to get out of there, to get out of the barracks in a hurry and hurry fast by the kapos to line up for the SS when the SS came.
Sometimes it took a couple of hours for them to come, but we were standing outside and huddling in the cold.
They did not make any selections in Ohrdruf.
I mean, they only took away the people that wouldn't work anymore.
They took them away from camp.
I mean, people were sick.
They couldn't work anymore.
They put them in-- they put them in the hospital area and the sick room.
And from there, they took them away.
No.
Only a men's camp.
No, nothing--
--Nothing really.
We were just talking about ourselves, that we shouldn't give up hope.
And the war is going to come to an end, and this is not going to-- you know, they're not going to be able to accomplish what they want to accomplish.
We did not know about the crematoria.
We didn't know any-- anything what was happening to the other people.
At that time, we were not aware what we know now, that in Auschwitz they made selections and they took them right into the crematoria.
No.
The only thing we knew that we were taking out stones out of the quarries.
There were-- there were some-- there were some tram, railroad tracks, that stones were filled, and if one of the trams overturned, and we had to pick it up.
And they were beating us to pick it up, and you know, you just had to pretend that you're doing your job.
Otherwise, they were just beating, just hitting us.
That was on the work site.
Not [? that I know of, ?] no.
Well, Buchenwald, it wasn't a tent outside.
We were in Buchenwald.
We were in the-- they had no room for us.
The barracks were all filled, and we were in the center.
For one day, they took us out of the center.
They loaded us on trains.
Like I mentioned before, they gave us bread, and they put us on the transport.
The only thing that I know, that after-- after being on transport for three weeks, out of 1,200 people, only about 300 or 400 survived.
Most of the Jews that were on the transport were killed.
As a matter of fact, it was an incident.
About two weeks after we were on the transport, we were at a clearing, and an order came from the SS-- all the Jews step down,  all the Jews step down.
We were in cattle cars.
We had some Russian prisoners and some Polish civilians that were with us on that transport.
And when they announced for the Jews to step down off the train, I knew that nothing good can come of it if they wanted Jews down.
So I stayed on the train.
I didn't step off.
And one of the Russians says, get down, you dirty Jew.
I started crying.
And an SS walked over to me.
He says, why are you crying?
I says, well, this guy pushed me off the train.
He says I am a dirty Jew.
He says, get back on the train.
I got back on the train.
They took about 800 Jews from the transport into the-- into the forest, and all we heard was the machine gun and screamings.
That was the end of the Jews on that transport.
And we were-- we were on that transport for another three, four days, [INAUDIBLE] when we got into Czechoslovakia.
And they had no place to go anymore, so we were stationed-- we were in a station, and the Czech people came to the fence of the yard, of the train yard, to find out if there were any Czech people.
And we volunteered.
We told them yes.
And they brought us food.
They brought  us some boiled potatoes.
And I remember an incident where a young-- young girl came with a pot of boiled potatoes, and she wanted to know if there was any Czechs.
So I-- I volunteered, and I told her.
She says, I have some potatoes for you.
Would you please take it?
I took the pot, and I put it on the ground.
And the minute I put down the pot, all the-- all the prisoners from the train jumped on the potatoes.
You know, they-- I didn't get any potatoes.
But the little girl started crying, because she didn't realize that people are starved.
And I bent down to pick up the-- to pick up the pot and to give it to her.
At that time, an  SS came over, and I was the only one left there, because everybody ran away with the potatoes.
And he hit me in the left side of the temple with the butt of the-- of the rifle.
I started bleeding.
I had a scarf on that was full of lice.
I put it into some muddy water, and I wrapped it on my head.
And that was-- that was the cure for me, without potatoes.
No, we had no markings on the  uniform.
No, nothing to identify us that we were Jewish.
We were just prisoners like everybody else in the same, you know.
I didn't have any other incident where I was pointed out being a Jew, you know.
Oh, yes.
We had-- we had yellow stars--
--on the uniform.
You know that, I don't know.
We didn't have-- we didn't have any markings on that transport.
I mean, just thinking back now, we didn't have any.
You couldn't-- you couldn't distinguish between Jews and non-Jews on that transport.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know whether we had-- everybody had yellow bands, but they were-- or there was a yellow mark on the back.
I don't remember, really.
But I know that there was no distinguish marking between Jews and non-Jews.
And talking about that transport, we came up again into this Czech village.

And the Czech people that were running the-- the-- the village were able to prevail on the Germans, because they had no place to go anymore, to find out if there are any Czech prisoners on the transport, to let them out-- to-- to let them out to their responsibility.
And one morning, they came and they started yelling on the train, anybody Czech, get off the train, get off the train.
So I got off the train.
Another 30-- another 30 kids got off the train.
I think they were all Jewish.
They started questioning us, first the Czech authorities--
--first the German authorities, and then the Czech authorities.
And they wanted to know where we are from and what our name was.
At that time, my name became Vladimir Burjan.
I took that name because Vladimir Burjan was a friend of mine I went to Czech school with.
He was a Czech official's son.
He was a very close friend, the same age as myself.
And I became Vladimir Burjan.
And the spelling of Vladimir Burjan is very simple-- V-L-A-D-I-M-I-R, second name Burjan, B-U-R-J-A-N.
I was taken in by a Czech family.
They knew that I was Jewish.
They treated me very well.
I was with that family for a couple of days until I got pneumonia, and I was taken away to a hospital.
And then I don't know what happened to me for two months, because I was very sick with high temperature in that hospital.
No, [SIGHS] I don't remember the name of the town.
I really don't.
I know it was in the [?
Sudetenland. ?]
Well, I understand that some of the SS were escaping from the train, at that [? last ?] transport, that some of the SS were-- there were less and less SS left on the transport.
Finding out later that they were escaping.
They were trying to run away themselves.
Yeah, at that time-- at that time, we already realized, because they told us they cannot go any further.
They have no place to go.
And some of  the SS were disappearing.
And we knew that's the end of the war, and we knew that liberation's close at hand.
Buchenwald, yes.
Nobody escaped the death march.
The only thing that happened at the death march, they sat down by the side of the road because they couldn't march anymore.
They couldn't take it anymore.
And that was where they got a bullet in their head.
Oh, yes.
People were insisting.
People were-- were-- were begging them not to sit down, to keep on going.
And people were helping other people that couldn't-- couldn't march, couldn't walk anymore.
They were practically carrying them to save their lives.
No.
I don't remember the name of the hospital.
I think they-- I think the town was Karlovy Vary, but I don't-- I'm not sure.
But that's, I think-- that's the best of my recollection as far as the town is concerned.
K-R-L-O-V-Y V-A-R-Y.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
At that time, in 1945, I was 22.
When I got well-- I don't know  how long it took me at hospital.
I went to Czech-- to-- to Prague to register.
Then I registered under my own name.
And at that point, they wanted to know what nationality you are.

Some people thought-- were very proud that they are Jews and they survived, and they put their nationality as Jewish, later realizing that that was a big mistake that they made, because once you were Jewish and you were born in Russia, you became a Russian citizen immediately.
I gave 'em-- I gave 'em the nationality Czech, and I became Czech on my identifications.
I don't know how   long I spent in Prague, but somehow I got on a train.
And we all were gravitating towards home to see who survived and who didn't survive in the concentration camps, and wound up in Loucky again.
I wasn't aware of being liberated because I was sick.
I was sick in the hospital.
You know, I only knew when I was getting better that the war is over,  and everybody is free.
I went to Prague.
I really don't know.
Not-- not any more than 10 days or so.
No.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Oh, yes.
One of-- one of the men-- boys that was liberated with me, in the same transport, later became my brother-in-law, Esther's husband, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Morry Eisderfer.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Well, I found nothing was left in our house.
It was empty.
Everything was stripped.
Nobody was living in there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Some of the neighbors wanted to know how come we weren't killed.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My present-- Etta's home became the center of our activities that was supported by the Joint Distribution Committee.
We set up a kitchen there, and anybody that came home, we were cooking there and feeding everybody.
At that time, we were waiting to hope, to hear from all the-- whoever survived.
One day, my two sisters came home.
I found out that they were in Budapest, and they got on the train, and they came to Loucky.
And then my beloved, Etta, came home one day with her two sisters.
Her brother was home already.
And it was a very happy reunion.
No, no, I never saw him.
But somehow, I found out where he was living, either through correspondence or through somebody else that saw him knew that I was using-- I used his name by liberation.
No.
No, none at all.
Oh, yes.
They were not very receptive to our survival.
And they were afraid whatever they stole from the Jews, that somebody's going to ask back for it.
So they-- they wanted to know how come we were not killed, that's all.
And the life-- the life was going on in Czechoslovakia, in Loucky at that time, and we knew that this is only temporary, and that we have to move on from there.

And my brother-in-law, who's my brother-in-law now, he was my friend, Etta's brother Joseph-- the two of us decided to go back to Czechoslovakia because we knew that we have somebody in the United States, in America.
My father was alive, and that we are going to hear from them, that they are going to want to take us out to the United States.
And we wanted to settle in Czechoslovakia, so my brother-in-law and I went back.
We came to a town of Zatec.
And--
Z-- Z with a [INAUDIBLE] A-T-E-C.
The local government assigned a beautiful home for us.
It was a big villa.
We found out later that it still belonged to a Jewish family by the name of Stern that were in New York.
Never was in touch with them.
I don't know what happened to them.
But-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --my brother-in-law and myself got lodging for ourselves.
We got a little house-- little house.
And we told the people that we are going to go home to Russia-- I mean, to the Carpathians, to take home-- to bring back my brother-in-law's three sisters and my two sisters.
And I went back.
I left Joe in Zatec, and I went back to Loucky to get them together to bring them back.
At that time, the borders were starting to close already, and there was no transportation from Munkacs to go to Czechoslovakia.
So we had to go by wagon over the border.
We were smuggled over the border into Koseca.
And at Koseca, we got a train and went to Prague.
Were happy times for the girls.
My sister-in-law Helen, and Elaine, and my two sisters, Magda and Esther, they had a lot of fun on the train.
And it was-- and I warned them, I says, please, don't make yourself known on the train.
Don't make any-- Don't make any moves that people should notice you, because the Russian soldiers were on those trains, and there were raping the young girls.
And these kids didn't want to listen to me.
So I took my belt, and I beat up my sister-in-law-- presently she was a young girl-- and my sister.
And then they were behaving.
So it was a big responsibility at that time.
And we got-- we got to Zatec.
And we were there, and we were living and waiting for the papers from the United States.
I was working in Zatec in a government office that was-- I was in charge of about six or eight people.
It was a-- it was called [NON-ENGLISH], which is a-- it's not a town.
It's an area office, area official office.
And all the-- I was in charge of all the mail that was coming in to that-- through the area from any official government offices.
Everything was coming through my hands.

And I was in a position to see when the Russian government was asking the Czech government to find the Schneiders, which was my wife and my-- and her two sisters and brother-- and bring them back to Russia, because they registered-- when they came out of the camp, they registered as Jewish nationality, and the Russian government wanted them back because they considered them as Russian citizens.
When I got notice from the chief of police from Prague that the Russian government was looking for them, I took it, and I put it in my drawer.
I didn't turn it over to anybody.
Then a few weeks later, they were-- I got another notice telling me that these people were not rounded up.
They know they live in Zatec, and a copy of the letter is going to the police.
And I got only one copy of the letter.
So that day, they had to leave Zatec.
They had to leave the town, because I knew that the police is going to pick them up.
They went to Karlovy Vary, and from there, we went to Prague.
We already had papers to come to the United States.
My wife, her two sisters, and brother-in-law had papers from their uncle, Uncle Henry.
And I and my two sisters have papers from my father, that he sent us the papers.
They bought flight tickets from Paris to New York for us, so those tickets were paid for already.
The only thing that we did not have, we did not have a set date when the flight was going to take us from Paris to New York.
And until-- until the flight-- until we had a designation of flight, we couldn't get any visas.
So when we got to Prague, I went to the Joint Distribution Committee, and I told 'em about our plight.
I told 'em that we are going to the United States.
And I told them that the Russians are looking for them, and we have to leave Czechoslovakia.
They put us on a train to Paris.
And in Paris, we stayed a few days until we came to America.
Quick clarification for the people who see this.
At the beginning of the war, Velike Loucky was part of Czechoslovakia.
Right.
Hungary, and it was called Nagylucska.
It was back to Velike Loucky.
It become-- it became Russia, which is presently the Ukraine.
I really don't remember how we got in touch and how he found out that we are alive.
But somehow he found out that we are alive.
And actually, from-- when we  were liberated in Prague, I knew his address, and I wrote him a letter.
And I told him that I'm going back to Loucky.
And he had-- this is how we got in touch.
And I told him who survived, that my sister survived.
And I also told him that-- to be in touch with Henry Davis, that his nephews-- nieces and nephew survived.
And we came to the United States within two days of each other.
I know that the rest of the family were killed.
My grandfather, who was 84 years old, was taken away from home.
My grandmother, who was 80 years old-- a picture in my head from my mother and my aunt that was living with us and her three little girls.
And my mother was of an age that she could have survived.
She wouldn't have been picked to be killed immediately.
But-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --she wanted to stay with her mother.
She wanted to stay and help her sister with the three little children.
So she told my sisters to go on the other side, and she'll see them later.
I heard it from my sisters.
